title: Mitigating Hallucination on Hallucination in RAG via Ensemble Voting
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) aims to reduce hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) by integrating external knowledge. However, RAG introduces a critical challenge: hallucination on hallucination," where flawed retrieval results mislead the generation model, leading to compounded hallucinations. To address this issue, we propose VOTE-RAG, a novel, training-free framework with a two-stage structure and efficient, parallelizable voting mechanisms. VOTE-RAG includes: (1) Retrieval Voting, where multiple agents generate diverse queries in parallel and aggregate all retrieved documents; (2) Response Voting, where multiple agents independently generate answers based on the aggregated documents, with the final output determined by majority vote. We conduct comparative experiments on six benchmark datasets. Our results show that VOTE-RAG achieves performance comparable to or surpassing more complex frameworks. Additionally, VOTE-RAG features a simpler architecture, is fully parallelizable, and avoids the problem drift" risk. Our work demonstrates that simple, reliable ensemble voting is a superior and more efficient method for mitigating RAG hallucinations.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable natural language understanding and reasoning capabilities [ 1 , 32 ] . However, their reliance on parametric knowledge introduces a critical challenge: hallucination, where the generated content deviates from factual correctness [ 20 , 17 ] . This issue severely limits their reliability, particularly in knowledge-intensive tasks where factual accuracy is paramount. To mitigate hallucinations, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has been proposed as a framework that integrates external knowledge retrieval to enhance LLM outputs [ 11 ] . By conditioning responses on retrieved documents, RAG reduces reliance on the model’s parametric knowledge, thereby aiming to improve factual correctness.

RAG introduces a new challenge: biased or erroneous retrieval results can mislead generation and amplify the LLM’s inherent hallucinations, producing what is called Hallucination on Hallucination [ 15 ] . Addressing this challenge requires optimizing the entire RAG pipeline — both retrieval and generation. In retrieval, incomplete or biased results mislead the generation stage; even when retrieval strategies are improved, the generator remains vulnerable to retrieval noise and misinformation.

Recent research has explored various optimization techniques to enhance the precision of the retrieval process. For instance, chat-driven interactions have been leveraged to improve text generation and interaction for person retrieval [ 37 ] , and context-aware representations with query enhancement have shown significant promise in reducing search ambiguity [ 39 ] . Furthermore, advancements in human vision-driven video representation [ 36 ] and hierarchical visual perception [ 38 ] demonstrate that incorporating multi-level semantic alignment can further stabilize retrieval in complex domains. Despite these advancements, existing RAG mitigations—such as iterative retrieval [ 33 , 30 , 10 ] and reflection during generation [ 2 ] —typically rely on single-agent self-optimization and therefore remain susceptible to inherent biases.

To address the two-stage challenge of Hallucination on Hallucination , a robust and efficient mechanism is required to improve both retrieval quality and generation fidelity. Instead of relying on a single, complex self-correction loop, we turn to the principles of ensemble methods. The “wisdom of the crowd,” realized through aggregation, is a powerful paradigm for enhancing robustness and mitigating errors from individual components. In multi-agent systems, this principle has proven to be highly effective. Recent research [ 7 ] provides strong empirical evidence that simple aggregation mechanisms, such as majority voting, are primary drivers of performance gains and decision accuracy in multi-agent LLM systems.

This ensemble voting approach is naturally suited to counteract the stochastic failure modes of RAG. It can (1) diversify the retrieval process to reduce bias and improve information coverage, and (2) stabilize the generation process against noise by establishing consensus among multiple independent answers.

Motivated by the power and simplicity of ensemble voting, we propose VOTE-RAG , a training‑free framework that applies this principle to both stages of the RAG pipeline. VOTE-RAG operates in two phases to target both sources of error. In the retrieval phase, Retrieval Voting has multiple agents generate diverse queries in parallel and aggregates all retrieved documents into a shared pool, broadening knowledge coverage and reducing bias. In the generation phase, Response Voting uses N N independent agents to produce answers from the same aggregated document pool, and the final answer is selected by simple majority vote.

Overall, our main contributions are summarized as follows: • We introduce VOTE-RAG , a novel training-free framework that integrates “Ensemble Voting” mechanisms into both the retrieval and generation phases of RAG. • We evaluate VOTE-RAG on multiple tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving retrieval reliability, enhancing reasoning robustness, and substantially reducing RAG-induced hallucinations.

sections.heading: II-ALimitations of Standard RAG
sections.text:
LLMs have demonstrated remarkable success across a wide range of tasks [ 1 , 32 ] .
However, they are prone to hallucinations, particularly when handling queries beyond their training data [ 20 , 17 ] .
To mitigate this issue, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances LLMs by incorporating external knowledge retrieval into the response generation process [ 11 ] .
The most straightforward approach involves using the initial input as a query to retrieve information from an external corpus [ 13 , 26 , 18 , 19 , 31 ] .
However, this standard RAG pipeline, which relies on a single query and a single generation pass, has key weaknesses. As noted in [ 15 ] , this process is vulnerable to ”Hallucination on Hallucination”: Retrieval Failure: A single-pass retrieval based solely on the initial input may fail to retrieve essential knowledge or may fetch biased, irrelevant information, leading to a flawed context. Generation Failure: Even if the correct information is retrieved, it is often mixed with noise. A single generation pass may be influenced by this noise, leading to an incorrect answer.
While methods like iterative retrieval [ 33 , 30 ] and self-reflection [ 2 ] exist, these approaches still rely on a single-agent reasoning process, which can be susceptible to its own inherent biases and failure modes [ 35 ] .

sections.heading: II-BMulti-Agent Ensembles and Voting
sections.text: To overcome the limitations of single-agent approaches, leveraging multi-agent systems is a promising direction. Aggregating outputs from multiple independent agents mitigates individual biases and substantially improves reasoning robustness [ 3 ] . Recent work has focused on finding the most effective and efficient aggregation mechanism; a critical study by Choi et al. [ 7 ] provides strong empirical evidence that the primary driver of gains is majority voting . They show that aggregating independent agent responses is a simple, robust, and reliable method that often outperforms more complex interaction strategies. This insight—that the strength of multi-agent systems lies in straightforward, parallel aggregation—underpins our approach. Rather than pursuing complex iterative self-correction, VOTE-RAG leverages ensemble voting to directly address the two-stage “Hallucination on Hallucination” problem in RAG.

sections.heading: II-CRobustness and Disambiguation in Complex Retrieval
sections.text: While text-based RAG deals with inherent ambiguity and noise in retrieved contexts, similar challenges have been extensively studied and addressed in complex multi-modal retrieval paradigms, such as Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) and Composed Video Retrieval (CVR). In these domains, queries consist of hybrid inputs (e.g., a reference image combined with a text modifier), making the retrieval process highly susceptible to noise, semantic misalignment, and focus shifts. Recent advancements in CIR and CVR provide valuable insights into robust retrieval mechanisms. For instance, approaches have been developed to explicitly parse fine-grained modification semantics [ 28 ] and bind entity-modification relations [ 27 ] to ensure highly precise retrieval targets. To mitigate the impact of inherent noise and ambiguity, researchers have proposed hierarchical uncertainty-aware disambiguation networks [ 6 ] and invariance-aware noise mitigation strategies [ 4 ] . Furthermore, addressing spatial and semantic focus shifts during retrieval has been explored through segmentation-based revision [ 5 ] , while other methods enhance overall retrieval accuracy by carefully separating shared and differential semantics [ 16 ] . The core principles of these techniques—specifically, rigorous semantic parsing, uncertainty disambiguation, and noise reduction—are highly relevant to addressing the ”Retrieval Failure” in standard RAG, providing a foundation for more resilient context-fetching mechanisms prior to generation.

sections.heading: IVTHE VOTE-RAG FRAMEWORK
sections.text:
In this section, we introduce VOTE-RAG, a general framework designed to enhance Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) through a structured ensemble voting mechanism. We first provide an overview of VOTE-RAG in Section IV-D , followed by a comprehensive discussion of its components. As illustrated in Fig. 1, VOTE-RAG consists of a two-stage voting framework that comprehensively addresses the hallucinations introduced by RAG in both the retrieval and generation stages.

Stage one is the Retrieval Voting , introduced in Section IV-A , while stage two is the Response Voting , discussed in Section IV-B . VOTE-RAG addresses the “Hallucination on Hallucination” problem via this structured two-stage process.

First, in the Retrieval Voting stage, multiple parallel agents analyze the initial question x x . Instead of relying on a single query, each agent a i a_{i} independently generates a unique query q i q_{i} . These are then aggregated into a comprehensive query pool Q ^ \hat{Q} , which increases retrieval breadth and minimizes single-query bias.

Second, in the Response Voting stage, all agents receive the same aggregated document set C Q ^ C_{\hat{Q}} and independently produce answer candidates y i y_{i} in parallel. The final response y ^ \hat{y} is selected using a majority voting function 𝒱 \mathcal{V} . This mechanism filters out stochastic hallucinations and ensures factual consensus, as demonstrated by the “3:3 score” example in the Fig. 1 overview.

sections.heading: IV-ARetrieval Voting
sections.text:
The retrieval step in standard RAG (Eq. 7 ) often suffers from single-query bias and limited coverage. VOTE-RAG replaces it with a parallel, aggregation-based procedure called Retrieval Voting : Q ^ , C Q ^ = f RetVote ​ ( x , ℛ , N ) , \hat{Q},\,C_{\hat{Q}}\;=\;f_{\mathrm{RetVote}}(x,\mathcal{R},N), (1) where N N is the number of retrieval agents, Q ^ \hat{Q} is the aggregated query pool, and C Q ^ C_{\hat{Q}} is the aggregated set of retrieved paragraphs used by the response stage.

We implement f RetVote f_{\mathrm{RetVote}} in four concise steps:

Ensemble Query Generation. Instead of a complex, serial debate, VOTE-RAG’s approach is parallel and aggregation-based. We instantiate N N independent ”Retrieval Agents” (e.g., N = 5 N=5 ).
Each agent a i a_{i} is prompted to independently analyze the original question x x and generate a query q i q_{i} that it determines is best suited to find the correct answer. q i = ℳ a i ​ ( x ) for ​ i = 1 , … , N q_{i}=\mathcal{M}_{a_{i}}(x)\quad\text{for }i=1,\dots,N (2)

Query Pool Aggregation. Once all N N queries are generated in parallel, they are aggregated into a single, comprehensive query pool Q ^ \hat{Q} . Q ^ = ⋃ i = 1 N { q i } \hat{Q}=\bigcup_{i=1}^{N}\{q_{i}\} (3) This process leverages the diverse perspectives of multiple agents to form a query strategy that is more robust than any single agent’s attempt.
The final aggregated query pool Q ^ \hat{Q} is then used to retrieve a comprehensive evidence set C Q ^ = ℛ ​ ( Q ^ , C , k ) C_{\hat{Q}}=\mathcal{R}(\hat{Q},C,k) , which is passed to the next stage.

sections.heading: IV-BResponse Voting
sections.text:
Even after optimizing retrieval with C Q ^ C_{\hat{Q}} , the model may still be influenced by noisy or conflicting information within the retrieved documents, leading to generation-stage hallucinations.
To address this, VOTE-RAG introduces Response Voting f ResVote f_{\text{ResVote}} , which leverages the ”wisdom of the crowd” to find the most robust and factually consistent answer. y ^ = f ResVote ​ ( x , C Q ^ , N ) \hat{y}=f_{\text{ResVote}}(x,C_{\hat{Q}},N) (4) where y ^ \hat{y} represents the final consensus response.

Symmetric Voting Structure. VOTE-RAG employs a fully symmetric and parallel setting. We instantiate N N independent ”Response Agents.”
Crucially, all N N agents receive the exact same input: the original question x x and the entire aggregated set of retrieved documents C Q ^ C_{\hat{Q}} from the previous stage.

Parallel Answer Generation. Each agent a i a_{i} then independently processes this information and generates its best answer y i y_{i} in parallel. y i = ℳ a i ​ ( x , C Q ^ ) for ​ i = 1 , … , N y_{i}=\mathcal{M}_{a_{i}}(x,C_{\hat{Q}})\quad\text{for }i=1,\dots,N (5)

Final Answer Aggregation. The set of N N independent answers { y 1 , y 2 , … , y N } \{y_{1},y_{2},\dots,y_{N}\} is collected. The final answer y ^ \hat{y} is determined by a majority vote aggregation function 𝒱 \mathcal{V} (e.g., selecting the most frequent answer string). y ^ = 𝒱 ​ ( y 1 , y 2 , … , y N ) \hat{y}=\mathcal{V}(y_{1},y_{2},\dots,y_{N}) (6) This ensemble approach is highly robust to individual agent failures. Even if one or two agents are misled by noise in C Q ^ C_{\hat{Q}} , the consensus of the majority is statistically much more likely to be correct. This avoids the high computational cost and potential ”problem drift” of iterative debate.

sections.heading: IV-Cintuition and error bounds
sections.text:
We give a short, easy-to-follow sketch explaining when majority voting helps in a two-stage RAG pipeline and when it fails. The goal is intuition and simple bounds that match the design choices in this paper.

Notation. Let N N be the number of agents. For agent i i define the indicator X i ∈ { 0 , 1 } X_{i}\in\{0,1\} where X i = 1 X_{i}=1 means agent i i is wrong. Write p = Pr ⁡ ( X i = 1 ) p=\Pr(X_{i}=1) for the (per-agent) error rate and S = ∑ i = 1 N X i S=\sum_{i=1}^{N}X_{i} . The majority vote is incorrect iff S ≥ m S\geq m with m = ⌈ N / 2 ⌉ m=\lceil N/2\rceil .

1) Exact expression. Under i.i.d. agents, P maj_err ​ ( N , p ) = ∑ k = m N ( N k ) ​ p k ​ ( 1 − p ) N − k . P_{\text{maj\_err}}(N,p)=\sum_{k=m}^{N}\binom{N}{k}p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}. This gives the exact failure probability for majority voting.

2) Exponential decay when agents are independent and better than random.
If p < 1 / 2 p<1/2 , Hoeffding’s inequality yields the simple bound P maj_err ​ ( N , p ) ≤ exp ⁡ ( − 2 ​ N ​ ( 1 2 − p ) 2 ) , P_{\text{maj\_err}}(N,p)\leq\exp\big(-2N(\tfrac{1}{2}-p)^{2}\big), so the error probability falls exponentially in N N . This explains why more (independent, competent) agents help quickly.

3) Effect of correlation. Independence is strong. Let ρ \rho be the average pairwise correlation between X i X_{i} and X j X_{j} ( i ≠ j i\neq j ). If Cov ​ ( X i , X j ) = ρ ​ p ​ ( 1 − p ) \mathrm{Cov}(X_{i},X_{j})=\rho p(1-p) , then Var ​ ( S ) = N ​ p ​ ( 1 − p ) ​ ( 1 + ( N − 1 ) ​ ρ ) . \mathrm{Var}(S)=Np(1-p)\big(1+(N-1)\rho\big). Chebyshev’s inequality gives the crude bound P maj_err ​ ( N , p , ρ ) ≤ p ​ ( 1 − p ) ​ ( 1 + ( N − 1 ) ​ ρ ) N ​ ( 1 2 − p ) 2 . P_{\text{maj\_err}}(N,p,\rho)\leq\frac{p(1-p)\big(1+(N-1)\rho\big)}{N(\tfrac{1}{2}-p)^{2}}. Positive correlation ( ρ > 0 \rho>0 ) weakens gains from increasing N N ; in the extreme ρ → 1 \rho\to 1 voting offers little benefit.

4) Two-stage composition (retrieval + response).
Let A A be the event that retrieval yields a sufficiently good evidence pool, and B B the event that response voting succeeds given good evidence. Then P fail ≤ Pr ⁡ ( ¬ A ) + Pr ⁡ ( ¬ B ∣ A ) . P_{\text{fail}}\leq\Pr(\neg A)+\Pr(\neg B\mid A). Modeling each stage with independent agents (with rates p r , p g p_{r},p_{g} and counts N r , N g N_{r},N_{g} ) leads to the heuristic bound P fail ≲ exp ⁡ ( − 2 ​ N r ​ ( 1 2 − p r ) 2 ) + exp ⁡ ( − 2 ​ N g ​ ( 1 2 − p g ) 2 ) . P_{\text{fail}}\lesssim\exp\big(-2N_{r}(\tfrac{1}{2}-p_{r})^{2}\big)+\exp\big(-2N_{g}(\tfrac{1}{2}-p_{g})^{2}\big). This highlights that both stages matter: improving retrieval (reducing p r p_{r} or its correlation) often has a large effect on the overall success.

5) Adversarial agents. If a fraction α \alpha of agents always err and the remaining agents have error p c p_{c} , the effective error is p eff = α + ( 1 − α ) ​ p c p_{\mathrm{eff}}=\alpha+(1-\alpha)p_{c} . Majority voting helps only when p eff < 1 / 2 p_{\mathrm{eff}}<1/2 , so the tolerated adversary fraction satisfies α < 1 / 2 − p c 1 − p c . \alpha<\frac{1/2-p_{c}}{1-p_{c}}.

We use these insights to justify: parallel diverse query generation (reduces p r , ρ r p_{r},\rho_{r} ), semantic clustering for answer aggregation, and optional verifier-based tie-breaking.

sections.heading: IV-DOverview
sections.text:
Formally, given a question x x , a standard RAG pipeline’s retriever ℛ \mathcal{R} retrieves top- k k supporting paragraphs C x k C^{k}_{x} from a knowledge corpus C C : C x k = ℛ ​ ( x , C , k ) C^{k}_{x}=\mathcal{R}(x,C,k) (7) Once retrieval concludes, the retrieved paragraphs C x k C^{k}_{x} and the question x x are used to prompt a large language model (LLM) ℳ \mathcal{M} , which then generates an answer y y : y = ℳ ​ ( x , C x k ) y=\mathcal{M}(x,C^{k}_{x}) (8)

As discussed in the introduction, this standard pipeline is vulnerable to Hallucination on Hallucination , where failures in Eq. 7 (retrieval) or Eq. 8 (generation) lead to compounded errors.
To mitigate these hallucinations, VOTE-RAG employs a two-stage ensemble voting mechanism: Retrieval Voting and Response Voting .
This voting-driven framework broadens the retrieved content and ensures a robust, factually-grounded consensus response.
The following subsections introduce the voting mechanisms for each stage separately.

sections.heading: V-ADatasets
sections.text:
We experimented on six benchmark datasets of three tasks. We follow previous work [ 21 ] and randomly select 500 examples from each dataset for testing.

Open-domain QA. Open-domain QA aims to answer a question in the form of natural language based on large-scale unstructured documents.
Here, we provide the model with only the questions without reference documents.
We choose three datasets for this task: NQ [ 25 ] , TriviaQA [ 23 ] , PopQA [ 29 ] .
For the evaluation metrics, we extract the final answer from the output using regular expressions and calculate the exact match (EM) and token-level F1 score.

Multi-hop QA. Multi-hop QA focuses on answering questions that require the ability to gather information from multiple sources and conduct multi-step reasoning to arrive at a comprehensive answer.
We choose the 2WikiMultihopQA [ 14 ] and HotpotQA [ 40 ] .
We also report EM and token-level F1 score for these datasets.

Commonsense Reasoning. Commonsense reasoning requires world and commonsense knowledge to generate answers.
We utilize the StrategyQA [ 12 ] to evaluate our model and other baselines.
We extract the yes/no response and compare it with the standard correct answer using EM.

sections.heading: V-BBaselines
sections.text:
We select Naive Gen , which is generated directly by an LLM, and MAD [ 8 ] as baselines that do not incorporate retrieval.
For retrieval-based baselines, we use Naive RAG , which represents the standard RAG method without any optimization.
Additionally, we compare VOTE-RAG with RAG optimization baselines, including IRCoT [ 33 ] , ITer-RetGen [ 30 ] , and FLARE [ 21 ] , which focus on improving the retrieval stage , as well as Self-RAG [ 2 ] and SuRe [ 24 ] , which enhance the generation stage.
Crucially, we also compare against DRAG [ 15 ] , the complex debate-based framework which our voting-based approach aims to simplify.

sections.heading: V-CImplementation Details
sections.text:
We selected Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct [ 9 ] as our base LLM model.Our implementation of VOTE-RAG and the baselines are built upon FlashRAG [ 22 ] , a Python toolkit designed for the reproduction and development of RAG-based systems.Following FlashRAG, we employed E5-base-v2 [ 34 ] as the retriever and used the widely adopted Wikipedia dump from December 2018 as the retrieval corpus .We retrieve top-3 paragraphs for each query for all retrieval-based methods.
For our reimplementation of the DRAG baseline, we follow its original settings: r = 3 r=3 maximum debate interactions and 3 3 agents (one for each role).
For VOTE-RAG (Ours), we set the number of parallel agents N N to 3 for both the Retrieval Voting and Response Voting stages, consistent with the ensemble size used in [ 15 ] .

sections.heading: VI-AComparison with Baselines
sections.text: We use Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct [ 9 ] as the base LLM and implement VOTE-RAG and the baselines on FlashRAG [ 22 ] . We employ E5-base-v2 [ 34 ] as the retriever and the Wikipedia dump. as the retrieval corpus, retrieving the top-3 paragraphs per query for all retrieval-based methods. For the DRAG baseline [ 15 ] we follow the original settings ( r = 3 r=3 maximum debate rounds and three agents, one per role). For VOTE-RAG (ours) we set the number of parallel agents to N = 3 N=3 in both the Retrieval Voting and Response Voting stages.

sections.heading: VI-BEfficiency Analysis
sections.text: We compare the efficiency of VOTE-RAG and DRAG on StrategyQA . DRAG requires 2.08 retriever calls and 10.36 LLM calls on average. While its adaptive termination reduces calls for simple queries, the multi-round response debate (avg. 7 calls) remains a latency bottleneck. In contrast, VOTE-RAG with N = 5 N=5 uses a fixed budget of 10 10 LLM calls. Although the total call count is similar to DRAG, VOTE-RAG’s non-iterative, fully parallelizable structure significantly reduces wall-clock latency under parallel inference. Furthermore, VOTE-RAG avoids the serial error amplification (“problem drift”) inherent in multi-round debates. While DRAG offers iterative adaptivity, VOTE-RAG provides a more predictable, low-latency execution with competitive robustness.

sections.heading: VIIConclusion
sections.text: In this paper, we address the challenge of “Hallucination on Hallucination” in RAG and introduce VOTE-RAG , a novel, training-free framework that uses parallel ensemble voting. VOTE-RAG employs a two-stage voting structure: (1) Retrieval Voting : multiple agents generate diverse queries in parallel to create a more comprehensive and less-biased retrieval pool; (2) Response Voting : multiple agents independently generate answers from the aggregated document pool, with a majority vote determining the final robust response. Our experiments across six datasets demonstrate that VOTE-RAG achieves robust factual accuracy comparable to more complex frameworks while remaining architecturally simpler and fully parallelizable. We recommend that future work continue to develop and refine VOTE-RAG-style ensemble mechanisms and de-emphasize complex interaction-based debate processes in favor of simple, efficient aggregation.

figures.caption: Figure 1: An overview of our VOTE-RAG framework. It leverages parallel ensemble voting to enhance retrieval breadth and generation robustness against ”Hallucination on Hallucination.”
